title: AI assisted coding in 2025
author: Juan Miguel Rodriguez
text:
AI assisted coding in 2025
The industry of software is in the middle of a revolution. Everybody is talking about AI, coding agents, and more recently ”Vibe coding”. At Clarity, we are working in improving our ways of work and practices to incorporate GenAI and improve our productivity.
For several months, most of the tech team uses some kind of coding assistant, but the usage varies a lot from one person to the next one. We are all experimenting and learning and so I will share some information from my personal experience with the current tools and how to configure and use them.
TLDR: the coding assistants have evolved a lot, autocomplete is just the first step, now we have CHOP, Cursor Rules, MCP and CLI agents.
Let’s talk a little bit about each of them:
CHOP
Coined by Steve Yegge from SourceGraph, CHOP stands for CHat Oriented Programing [1] This means that, instead of going to Google, StackOverflow, or the documentation page, you ask in the chat to an LLM. Most of the times the answer will be enough to unblock you, in the best case scenario the LLM will provide a full solution to your question.
This is becoming the “de facto” way of working for most developers. As the data shows, the number of questions asked in StackOverflow has gone down dramatically from 2021:
Having the knowledge of the models just one click away helps with many recurrent tasks of development. Even when you don’t ask for the code directly, the ability to ask anything about your code and have an answer in seconds, without ads, distractions or context switching is a great productivity boost for development. LLMs can help with:
- Learning how to implement something
- Rubber ducking
- Explaining functions/classes in the codebase
- Architecture charts, sequence diagrams of our code.
- Code review, performance suggestions
If we use the “Agent” mode, that all these plugins and IDEs already provide, we can even ask directly for the code, documentation, tests, design documents.
This is starting to work really well, specially with the last round of thinking models: Claude 3.7, Gemini 2.5 pro and o4-mini are able to perform full tasks including tool use, making patches to the code and creating files. For some people, specially if you work in small repos, writing code is no longer needed. You explain the task to the agent, add the proper context, review the agent changes and keep iterating.
There are many guides out there on “how to ask” or what is usually known as “prompting”. We are all learning and sometimes we read about some hacks that can help get better results, but it is never clear if those apply from one model to the next and it all sounds very unreliable.
My advice is to follow the documentation form the companies that produce this models and keep it simple while providing as much context as possible. [2][3][4] The models are getting to a point where you can talk almost like you would talk to a coworker that needs to take over your task.
The possibilities are endless and the bottleneck is increasingly the amount of context that we can provide to the model.
Context Sharing: Cursor Rules and MCP
To fix the context problem the solution to this day (May 2025, this can change in the next months) is storing your prompts alongside your code in the form of “rules”. Cursor was the first to adopt this strategy with the .cursorrules file that then turned into a whole folder so we can have different files for different contexts.
Each file has a bit of front matter at the top that allows to define to which files should Cursor apply this context and a description for the Agent mode so it can have an overview of all the files without loading all of them in the context. [5] This allows us to add context specific for tests, or for CSS files or for different services in a monorepo.
But beyond some rules and instructions or documentation there is the problem of accessing external tools for context. We usually store documentation, acceptance criteria, design documents or meeting notes outside of the repo, in external applications. The next improvement for adding this context to the conversation is MCP servers. MCP stands for Model Context Protocol.
MCP servers provide tools for the model to access external sources. This include external systems like Jira, Gitlab, Slack, that are already created and only need a couple of lines of configuration to work. Or internal systems that you can add by creating your own company-wide or local servers.
MCP is a way to standardize the way in which the servers expose those tools to the Client. Under the hood, the code is very similar to the ReAct pattern [6] The flow of the requests goes like this:
- The client gets the list of available tools from the server
- The user query is sent to the LLM along with tool descriptions
- The LLM decides which tools (if any) to use
- The client executes any requested tool calls through the server
- Results are sent back to the LLM along with the previous context and request.
- The LLM provides a natural language response or decides to use another tool (go to 4).
- The response is displayed to the user
Tools can be used to retrieve data, but also to execute commands, this flow around the LLM is what makes the system an agent able to perform actions and get observations from the world. IDE Agents like cursor already have some tools that they can use to run commands inside the project folder to get information about the repo, run tests or ejecute project commands.
You can read more about MCP Servers in the Cursor documentation [6].
CLI Agents
The next step in automation using foundational models is CLI Agents. They are similar to the IDE “Agent mode” but in this case the focus is in allowing the agent to execute commands on your behalf from the command line.
You don’t even need and IDE and reviewing the changes that the agent does to your project is a bit more difficult. So it implies that you are giving the agent more autonomy and you have to trust it more.
This agents usually default to asking the user for any destructive change or command execution, but you quickly find out that if you have to say “yes” to every command and modification, you are the bottleneck, and that’s why this tools provide flags and config options to set different grades of autonomy to the agent.
In the last months several tools have appeared with similar propositions but depending on your prefered model or the company policies about which one it is a trusted partner you will prefer one or the other:
- Anthropic has Claude Code
- OpenAI has CodexCLI
- Aider and SmolAgents are two open source alternatives that allow you more control and personalization with a little more work.
What’s Next: Trends and Predictions
As these tools become more integrated into our workflows, they are also shaping the future landscape of software development. It is really difficult to predict anything in the current market explosion of options, models, IDEs, Agents, and tools that appear every day but I will give my opinion on the tendencies I see:
- This is the worst versions of agents: models, MCP and IDEs are rapidly evolving. Keep an eye open, what today looks imposible tomorrow can be trivial.
- Small tools are becoming virtually free: build tools, short scripts, small apps, automating manual processes. All of this is within the reach of non technical people with a bit of curiosity. These tools are not yet popular outside the tech circles, but their adoption will keep growing because the barrier of entry is very low. We already see product managers and executives that have not written a line of code in their life claiming they can create prototypes and iterate on them thanks to these tools.
- Services and small apps will become more popular. As AI can work better in small repositories, the code it is easier to review and AI can also help with infra complexity. This is a new incentive to keep the tendency towards microservices, serverless functions and edge computing moving forward.
- People are going to get used to asking the computer what they want. Complex interfaces will become less popular. Design is already being transformed by this tendency, this will reach more and more verticals in the next months and years. One example that I love to point to is https://computer.tldraw.com/ where you can write complex workflows and each of the pieces are just prompts for models, similar to the image workflows that we see in AI tools for design and video generation.
- Development will shift:
We’re beginning to see that certain traditional skills — like deep language-specific expertise — may become less critical in some AI-assisted workflows:
- Language proficiency: jumping languages is much easier now. When you already know a the foundational elements of programing you can read most languages and supervise the model output at the same time that you ask questions and learn the language.
- Ability to turn a fuzzy description into a prototype. This is what people are already calling “Vibe coding”. Agents are able to create small prototypes and iterate a bit on them.
- Knowledge breadth. We have gone from books to Google search and digital documentations and now to LLMs. The cost of getting the information that you need at the right moment has decreased a lot, and the value of already knowing that information goes down as a consequence.
- And probably many more that I didn’t think of or that will get less important as LLMs capabilities improve. Touch typing? Personally I prefer to write the prompt because I usually are not able to articulate it aloud, but maybe we get use to that in the future. - Other skills will become more important: We have to find out what are those skills and keep adapting.[8][9] Some personal suggestions: product engineering, code and design review, critical thinking, being able to communicate and design contracts between different layers of the stack. Integrating all this tools across companies and teams is going to take time and it will make evident new bottlenecks. Intentionality, keeping the whole picture in mind and critical thinking are still on the human side and we will need to keep applying the same engineering practices to the new challenges that will appear as a result of this changes. Knowing what to build, knowing your market and your users, understanding trade offs and experimenting and learning are even more important when execution is cheaper.
We’re excited about how these tools will shape the future of software development. We’ll continue to share our learnings as we experiment and evolve. — Juan Miguel Rodriguez, Senior Software Engineer at Clarity.
Special thanks to the colleagues that reviewed the article and helped to improve it with their comments and suggestions: , , ,
References
[1] CHOP: https://sourcegraph.com/blog/the-death-of-the-junior-developer
[2] Prompt engineering: https://help.openai.com/en/articles/6654000-best-practices-for-prompt-engineering-with-the-openai-api
[3] Prompt engineering: https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/prompt-engineering/overview
[4] Prompt engineering: https://www.augmentcode.com/blog/best-practices-for-using-ai-coding-agents
[5] Cursor Rules: https://docs.cursor.com/context/rules
[6] ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models https://react-lm.github.io/
[7] MCP: https://modelcontextprotocol.io/introduction
[8] https://platforms.substack.com/p/the-many-fallacies-of-ai-wont-take
